claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your question! To help determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child - Alex, who's 17 and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 17, I need to ask: Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, or an apprenticeship)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex isn't in college or doing A-levels or anything like that, but they have started an apprenticeship in England. They're training to be an electrician.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. I need to clarify one important detail: Has Alex started working as part of this apprenticeship for 24 hours or more per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work 24 hours or more a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few more questions to ensure I assess your eligibility correctly.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Just to confirm - is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official guidance I've reviewed, I need to inform you of an important rule regarding apprenticeships in England.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: While Alex is 17 and lives with you, and you would normally be able to continue claiming Child Benefit for a child under 20 who is in approved education or training, there is a specific rule that affects apprenticeships. According to the official guidance, **you stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England**.

Since Alex has started an apprenticeship in England (training to be an electrician), this means Child Benefit entitlement ends, even though Alex is working less than 24 hours per week.

If you were previously receiving Child Benefit for Alex, you should inform HMRC about this change in circumstances, as the apprenticeship affects your entitlement.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and lives with you. According to the eligibility rules, you can normally continue to claim Child Benefit for a child aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. However, the rules specifically state that 'You'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England.' Since Alex has started an apprenticeship in England (training to be an electrician), this disqualifies you from receiving Child Benefit for Alex, regardless of the fact that Alex works less than 24 hours per week. The apprenticeship rule is a specific exception that ends Child Benefit entitlement.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old child Alex, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. While the claimant would normally be responsible for Alex and meet the basic criteria, Alex has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, starting an apprenticeship in England is one of the specific circumstances that stops Child Benefit entitlement, even though Alex is under 20 and works less than 24 hours per week. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

The apprenticeship rule is a specific exception that ends Child Benefit entitlement.

While the claimant would normally be responsible for Alex and meet the basic criteria, Alex has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, starting an apprenticeship in England is one of the specific circumstances that stops Child Benefit entitlement, even though Alex is under 20 and works less than 24 hours per week. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
